revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Jōe]][[en:Jōe]][[es:Jōe]][[fr:Jōe]][[ja:浄衣]][[mk:Каригину]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Jōe]][[en:Jōe]][[es:Jōe]][[fr:Jōe]][[ja:浄衣]][[mk:Каригину]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Jōe]][[en:Jōe]][[es:Jōe]][[fr:Jōe]][[ja:浄衣]][[mk:Каригину]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Jōe]][[en:Jōe]][[es:Jōe]][[fr:Jōe]][[ja:浄衣]][[mk:Каригину]]
{{Short description|Religious garment worn in Japan}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|thumb|250px|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} (right) wearing a {{transl|ja|jōe}}]] {{nihongo3|sometimes translated as "pure cloth"||'''Jōe'''}} is a garment worn in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} by people attending religious ceremonies and activities, including {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism in Japan}} and [[Shinto]] related occasions. The {{transl|ja|jōe}} is essentially a white {{transl|ja|[[:en:Kariginu|Kariginu]]}}, traditional hunting robes worn by nobles during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.
Though both Shinto and Buddhist priests wear {{transl|ja|jōe}} to rituals, laymen also occasionally wear the {{transl|ja|jōe}}, such as when participating in pilgrimage such as the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|qid=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}. The garment is usually white or yellow, and is made of {{ill|Linen|en|Linen|qid=Q1426327|lt=Linen|1=Linen}} or {{ill|Silk|en|Silk|qid=Q37681|lt=Silk|1=Silk}} depending on its type and use.
Shinto priests who wear the {{transl|ja|jōe}} usually wear it with a peaked {{ill|Cap|en|Cap|qid=Q6147804|lt=Cap|1=Cap}} known as {{transl|ja|tate-eboshi}}, alongside an outer tunic - the {{transl|ja|jōe}} proper - an outer robe called {{transl|ja|jōe no sodegukuri no o}}, an undergarment known as the {{transl|ja|hitoe}} (lit. "unlined" or "one-layer"), ballooning trousers called {{transl|ja|sashinuki}} or {{transl|ja|nubakama}} (a variant of the {{transl|ja|{{ill|Hakama|en|Hakama|qid=Q164668|lt=Hakama|1=Hakama}}}}), and a girdle called {{transl|ja|jōe no ate-obi}}. A priest may also carry a ceremonial wand known as a {{transl|ja|[[:en:Haraegushi|Haraegushi]]}}, or another style of baton known as a {{transl|ja|[[:en:Shaku (Japanese ritual baton)|shaku]]}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
== External links == *[http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_j.html#joe Basic terms of Shinto (with illustration)] *[http://www.iz2.or.jp/english/fukusyoku/wayou/10.htm Courtier in Shinto ceremonial robe (with illustration)] *{{cite encyclopedia |encyclopedia=Yahoo! Encyclopedia (Shogakukan 日本大百科全書) |title=Jo-e (浄衣) |url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%B5%84%E8%A1%A3/ |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120708173017/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E6%B5%84%E8%A1%A3/ |url-status=dead |archive-date=2012-07-08 |access-date=2009-04-10 |publisher=Shogakukan |language=ja }}
